T1.reason: O0 (generate a serialized solver transcript where useful context for token t_i is the textual prefix t_{<i}) and O1 (generate a compact dependency-layered certificate where useful context is problem + declared ancestors, with same-depth cells independently denoisable) are clearly specified and structurally distinct modeling objects; the shift is from 'predict a narrative' to 'predict a dependency DAG of locally checkable atoms'.

T2.reason: O1 differs from O0 on four axes simultaneously: (1) training data format (DAG-structured canvas vs serial text), (2) mask distribution (cell/layer/ancestor-only masks vs i.i.d. token masks), (3) auxiliary loss (dependency-selective invariance enforcing peer-independence and ancestor-dependence), (4) the target object the model learns to emit (a proof/computation memo with typed cells and declared deps vs a left-to-right thought trace). This is far beyond a hyperparameter change.

T3.reason: Section 5 lists 6 concrete pre-training falsifiers (parallelizable mass <25-30%, ancestor-only recoverability bad, stabilization = text order, result tokens not earlier than filler, certificate compiler fails >5-10%, brevity baseline ties) AND Section 4 provides a 5-step DPC with numeric GO thresholds (PMASS >=0.30-0.40, ARNESS_resid low, overlapping stabilization windows, Dprefix <=0.2-0.3 nats/token). All are checkable before any training GPU-hour.

T4.reason: NAP (arXiv:2602.23225) is explicitly named as the closest rival with a stated distinction: NAP = K full independent reasoning paths + summary with standard masked CE (internal self-ensemble); PFC = one non-redundant dependency DAG with locally checkable atoms + dependency-selective invariance loss that explicitly separates ancestor-dependence from peer-invariance. The distinction is structural (redundant ensemble vs non-redundant certificate; standard loss vs conditional-independence loss). Secondary rivals Diffusion of Thoughts (2402.07754) and ThreadWeaver (2504.15466) are also named and distinguished. Caveat: the NAP distinction narrows if in practice DAGs have wide layers with near-full independent sub-solutions (converging toward an ensemble).

T5.reason: PFC is training-based SFT on LLaDA-8B (not a frozen post-hoc head); uses standard discrete masked diffusion (not He-line continuous-flow); explicitly matches compute budget (not a compute-allocator/always-deepen); measures verified accuracy under same budget (not wall-clock EVALUATION framing); trains the model (not a frozen-only intervention on DiffusionGemma). All five FORBIDDEN items are avoided.

T6.reason: Section 4 provides the Parallel Frontier Recoverability Contract — a 5-step pre-training DPC (parse atoms, infer dependency graph, compute stabilization times from logs, forward-pass counterfactual, compression estimate) with concrete numeric GO conditions (PMASS, ARNESS_resid, overlapping stabilization, Dprefix/Dpeer thresholds). This is runnable on existing LLaDA-8B logs before any training.

honesty_gaps: The certificate compiler (Section 2, data construction steps 1-7) is described as 'partly heuristic' and its quality is critical to the entire approach — if it cannot produce locally-checkable, answer-preserving certificates at >90-95% pass rate, PFC degrades to formatted terse CoT with extra markup. The compiler is itself an unvalidated dependency and should be tested on a sample of 20-30 problems BEFORE the DPC probe runs.

The claim 'this helps dLLMs more than AR models' (Section 3) is asserted as intuition ('a masked dLLM can denoise cell A, B, C, and D together') but is not falsifiable from the DPC and is not part of the pre-training contract — it should be stated as a hypothesis, not a fact.

DPC Step D (forward-pass counterfactual) is marked 'optional' but is the STRONGEST pre-training signal — it directly tests whether the base model can already predict sub-results from the true dependency set rather than the serialized prefix. Making it optional weakens the DPC; if Step D is skipped, Steps A-C alone could give false GO signals because they measure log-derived properties (stabilization times, graph structure) rather than the model's actual conditional distributions.

The mask distribution percentages (40/25/20/10/5) and role/noise weighting scheme are presented as the design but are actually tunable hyperparameters whose values are unjustified. A sensitivity analysis or ablation plan should be pre-registered, otherwise the design has a hidden search space that could mask whether the object-shift matters vs the hyperparameter tuning.

The design targets LLaDA-8B but the goal directive's primary substrate is DiffusionGemma-26B-A4B MoE. The transferability of DPC findings from LLaDA-8B logs to the eventual DiffusionGemma deployment is assumed but not discussed. If the parallel frontier signal is present in LLaDA-8B but absent in DiffusionGemma (different architecture, different denoising dynamics), training results may not transfer.

The 'intended effect is not faster inference' caveat (Section 3) contradicts the project's stated goal of making diffusion-LM reasoning 'more token-efficient per step' — the design should clarify whether the claim is about tokens-per-step efficiency, verified-accuracy-at-matched-budget, or both, and pre-commit to one primary metric.

nap_distinction_assessment.detail: The distinction is structurally genuine on two axes: (1) PFC uses one non-redundant dependency DAG vs NAP's K redundant full paths (non-ensemble vs ensemble), and (2) PFC adds dependency-selective invariance loss vs NAP's standard masked CE. However, the distinction NARROWS under two scenarios: (a) if MATH/AIME dependency DAGs are mostly chains (low parallelizable mass), PFC degrades toward serialized certificate — which IS a preregistered baseline in the doc (Serialized Certificate SFT), so this is correctly handled; (b) if the dependency-selective invariance loss contributes nothing over standard masked CE on the same certificate format, PFC's novelty over NAP collapses to 'data format + terse compression', which is much closer to occupied territory. The design does preregister baseline B (Serialized Certificate SFT with standard masked CE) which would catch scenario (b). Overall: the distinction holds as designed but depends critically on the dependency-selective invariance loss being the active ingredient.

dependency_selective_invariance_overlap.detail: The specific formulation — P(v | problem + ancestors) should be approximately invariant to same-layer peer visibility, while P(v | problem + ancestors including u) should improve over P(v | problem + ancestors without u) — is a conditional-independence enforcement loss applied at the reasoning-atom level within masked diffusion LM training. Prior work on conditional independence in generative models (contrastive/disentanglement objectives, copula-based token dependencies in discrete diffusion, structural causal model losses) operates at different granularity (token-level or representation-level, not reasoning-atom-level) or in different settings (VAEs, GANs, not masked diffusion). The closest occupied methods are (1) CoDD/ADJUST energy-based dependency modeling in discrete diffusion (token-level, not atom-level), (2) NAP's parallel-path forcing (enforces path-level independence, not cell-level conditional independence given ancestors). No direct occupant found. However, the PRINCIPLE (teach a model that certain outputs are conditionally independent given certain inputs) is well-established — the novelty is in the application to masked-diffusion reasoning certificates, not in the loss concept itself.

claim_language_restrictions: Do NOT claim 'novel loss function' for dependency-selective invariance — the conditional-independence principle is well-known; the novelty is its application to reasoning-atom-level structure in masked diffusion LM training. Claim at most 'a new application of conditional-independence enforcement to dependency-structured reasoning certificates in discrete diffusion LMs'.

Do NOT claim PFC is 'fundamentally different from NAP' — the distinction is structural but moderate; claim 'PFC differs from NAP in using a non-redundant dependency DAG with a dependency-selective auxiliary loss, rather than redundant parallel paths with standard training'.

Do NOT claim parallel speedup or wall-clock gains — the design explicitly states 'the intended effect is not faster inference as the primary metric'; claim only 'higher verified accuracy under the same token/step budget'.

Do NOT describe the certificate compiler as 'automated' or 'reliable' until it is validated at >90-95% fidelity on a held-out problem set — describe it as 'a partly heuristic compiler whose fidelity is a pre-training prerequisite (falsifier #5)'.

DPC Step D MUST be run (not optional) before any GO decision — downgrade it from 'optional' to 'required for GO' in the pre-registration, because Steps A-C alone cannot distinguish 'the logs show graph structure' from 'the model can actually use graph structure for prediction'.

Before any training, run the Serialized Certificate SFT baseline (baseline B in the doc) as the FIRST training experiment — if PFC does not beat serialized certificate, the parallel structure claim is dead regardless of whether it beats standard CoT.

The mask distribution (40/25/20/10/5) and role weighting must be pre-registered as fixed before training, or an explicit ablation schedule must be declared — do NOT tune these post-hoc and then claim the object-shift, rather than the hyperparameters, caused the gain.
